skill.name: performance
skill.description: Performance analysis and optimization guidance for applications, databases, frontend assets, React rendering, load testing, and capacity planning.
skill.summary: Analyze bottlenecks, improve runtime performance, and plan safe load testing.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are false positives from Markdown code fences, examples, and performance terminology. The remaining risk is operational: the skill covers load testing, external HTTP requests, and local build-analysis commands, which require explicit authorization and controlled targets.

medium_findings.title: Load Testing Can Generate External Traffic
medium_findings.description: The network findings are true positives in context. The skill includes k6 and Artillery examples that send HTTP traffic and guidance to increase load gradually. This is legitimate performance work, but it can disrupt services if used against systems without permission.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples clearly create HTTP traffic and describe load-test execution. The risk depends on user authorization and target selection, so this is operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Documented Local Build Commands Need User Approval
medium_findings.description: The external command findings are mostly false positives from Markdown fences, but the skill also includes shell examples for bundle analysis. These commands are normal developer tooling, yet they should only run in a trusted project after user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of npm and npx command examples. They are not hidden execution logic, but running them can execute project scripts or downloaded packages.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography matches do not indicate unsafe crypto use. The cited content discusses performance concepts, templates, or placeholders, and no implementation performs hashing, encryption, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines found descriptive performance text, a note about asynchronous crypto operations, and report placeholders. No weak algorithm or security-sensitive crypto implementation was present.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Findings Are Benign Code Splitting Examples
low_findings.description: The script findings are false positives. The dynamic import examples are standard React lazy loading and route splitting patterns, with static relative module names and no user-controlled import path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import targets are fixed local modules used for bundle optimization examples. I found no evidence of runtime loading from user input or remote URLs.

low_findings.title: Example URLs Are Placeholders
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are low risk because they use api.example.com in documentation examples. They do not exfiltrate data, include secrets, or contact an author-controlled endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domain is a reserved example domain and appears only inside sample load-test configuration. The broader network load-testing behavior still needs user authorization.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Flags Are Performance Terminology
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance matches are false positives. The cited lines describe layout, query optimization, and memory cleanup practices, not host discovery, network scanning, or environment enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are normal performance guidance for frontend layout and database optimization. I found no evidence of commands or instructions for reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Load-Test Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: Sample load-test scripts issue HTTP GET requests. This pattern is safe only for authorized targets and controlled request rates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and relevant to the skill purpose. It is dangerous only when aimed at unauthorized or production systems without controls.

dangerous_patterns.title: Stress-Test Ramp-Up Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to increase load progressively and monitor systems. This is legitimate testing methodology, but it can cause denial-of-service conditions if misused.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly describe ramping load. Intent appears legitimate, but operational impact can be high without authorization.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Runner Command Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The bundle analysis example uses npx, which can execute local or fetched package code depending on environment and package manager behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is a standard analyzer invocation and not malicious. The risk is execution of project scripts or packages in an untrusted repository.

content.value_statement: Slow systems lose users and make capacity planning harder. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code identify bottlenecks, improve performance, and design controlled load tests.
actual_capabilities: Analyzes application, database, frontend, and React performance patterns.
Creates performance audit reports with bottlenecks, root causes, and recommendations.
Guides load testing, stress testing, and capacity planning workflows.
Suggests React optimizations for memoization, lazy loading, and long lists.
Explains Core Web Vitals targets and common frontend improvements.
Identifies monitoring metrics such as response time, throughput, CPU, and memory.
limitations: It does not prove performance gains without real measurements from the target system.
Load tests require explicit permission for the target environment.
Recommendations may need adaptation for each framework, database, and deployment model.
It is not a substitute for production observability or incident response processes.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Slow APIs
use_cases.description: Find slow endpoints, database bottlenecks, connection pool issues, and practical fixes.

use_cases.title: Improve Frontend Responsiveness
use_cases.description: Review React rendering, bundle size, lazy loading, and Core Web Vitals problems.

use_cases.title: Plan Capacity Safely
use_cases.description: Design controlled load tests, interpret throughput limits, and estimate scaling needs.

prompt_templates.title: Find Basic Bottlenecks
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this application for obvious performance bottlenecks. Focus on response time, database queries, memory usage, and frontend loading.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize React Rendering
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React feature for unnecessary re-renders, expensive calculations, bundle size issues, and Core Web Vitals risks.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Load Test Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a load test plan for this API. Include safe ramp-up stages, metrics to monitor, stop conditions, and expected analysis output.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Capacity Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use these performance results to create a capacity planning report. Identify limits, scaling options, risks, and next measurements needed.

output_examples.input: The users endpoint has slow p95 latency and high database time.
output_examples.output: The report identifies a likely query bottleneck, lists the metrics to confirm it, and recommends index review, query consolidation, and before-and-after measurements.

output_examples.input: A React dashboard feels slow when filters change.
output_examples.output: The analysis separates render cost, derived data calculation, and bundle loading concerns, then recommends memoization, list virtualization, and profiler checks.

output_examples.input: We need to test 1,000 concurrent users before launch.
output_examples.output: The plan defines an authorized test environment, progressive load stages, stop conditions, resource metrics, and a capacity summary format.

best_practices: Measure baseline performance before making optimization changes.
Run load tests only against systems you own or have permission to test.
Change one variable at a time and verify results with repeatable metrics.
anti_patterns: Do not run stress tests against public or production systems without approval.
Do not optimize code based only on intuition without profiling data.
Do not add memoization, caching, or concurrency without checking correctness and freshness needs.

faq.question: Can this skill run load tests automatically?
faq.answer: It can help create and analyze load tests. Any execution should require user approval and an authorized target.

faq.question: Does it support React performance work?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers memoization, lazy loading, bundle analysis, long list rendering, and Core Web Vitals.

faq.question: Can it diagnose database performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides review of slow queries, missing indexes, N+1 patterns, connection pools, and caching.

faq.question: Is it safe for production systems?
faq.answer: Analysis is safe, but load tests can affect availability. Use staging or approved production windows.

faq.question: What tools does it mention?
faq.answer: It references k6, Artillery, JMeter, Locust, React Profiler, and bundle analysis tools.

faq.question: Does it guarantee faster performance?
faq.answer: No. It provides structured diagnosis and recommendations. Real gains require measurement and verification.
